title: That 1970s Bug That Still Haunts Modern AI
author: Sumit Shaw
text:
Member-only story
That 1970s Bug That Still Haunts Modern AI
“Old bugs never die — they just evolve.”
In 1971, a subtle race condition brought down the launch of a NASA software system. In 2025, nearly the same logic flaw showed up again — but this time, buried inside the auto-generated code of an AI model used by millions.
How can a 50-year-old bug resurface in bleeding-edge AI systems?
The answer: we’ve gotten better at generating code, but not necessarily at understanding it.
The Original Bug: A Brief History
The bug was buried in a multi-threaded communication system. Two signals — “ready” and “acknowledge” — could fire in the wrong order due to shared memory issues. On paper, it seemed flawless. In real time? Crash.
This wasn’t exotic. It was a classic race condition — where two parts of a program access shared data without proper synchronization.
The fix? Introduce mutexes or semaphores to prevent unsafe simultaneous access. Old school, but effective.
How It Reappeared — 50 Years Later
Recently, I asked an LLM (large language model) to build a Python script with basic file I/O and multithreading. The output worked… until it didn’t.
